title: Chronal Coordinates
description: Find the largest finite Voronoi region using Manhattan distance, then find the region where total distance to all coordinates is below a threshold.
writeup:
## Approach

The input is a list of 2D coordinates. Both parts scan a bounding-box grid and compute Manhattan distances from each cell to every coordinate.

## Part 1

This is a Manhattan-distance Voronoi diagram:

- For each grid cell, find the closest coordinate.
- If two or more coordinates tie for closest, the cell belongs to no one.
- Any coordinate that owns a cell on the bounding-box edge has an infinite region (it would keep expanding beyond the grid).
- Count the area of each non-infinite region and return the largest.

The bounding box of the input coordinates is sufficient — any finite region must be fully contained within it.

## Part 2

For each grid cell, sum the Manhattan distances to all coordinates. Count how many cells have a total below 10,000.

- The answer region is roughly centered among the coordinates, so the same bounding box covers it.
- O(w × h × n) where n is the number of coordinates — fast enough for typical AoC inputs.

## Code Highlights

- `sorted(...)[0]` and `[1]` in one call detects both the closest owner and whether there's a tie.
- Infinite-region detection: any owner that appears on the border of the bounding box must be infinite.
- Visualizer renders the full Voronoi color map, then dims infinite regions, then shows the safe zone in green for part 2.
